speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I think one of the most important cases historically that's been decided in the courts here in Moscow was the case of Day versus Stenger. Now, this is a case that most people have forgotten about but involve the property line or the property lines on the properties on the east side of Main Street. Now Jerry Day then lived in his big house, big, big wooden house up in the east part of Moscow on the hill.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And his neighbor was a fine enough man by the name of Stenger. Now they got in a dispute about the boundary line, and in order to settle it, Jerry Day brought an action. Of, action against Stenger to establish his boundary line. Now, it developed in the trial of the case that when Moscow was first surveyed, the main, township line ran down Main Street, supposedly north and south, but it developed later that it wasn't on a true north and south.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: It was a little bit off. And of course, all the property lines and for the additions for the all the east side of Moscow were drawn at right angles to the, the center line down at the on Main Street. Well, of course, if it was a little bit off here, it would gradually, the further you got east, the greater the divergence would be from the true 90 degree angle from what was north and south.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, the controversy between Day and Stenger was the result of that. And the case was tried out in the district court before Judge Steele without a jury, because the facts were pretty well, pretty well, there's a little dispute in the facts. Now, Mr. Orin, was the attorney for Stanger, and, I was then practicing with him, although we have not yet become partners.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But I worked hard and on the case with him, and he tried the case. I helped collect some of the evidence and they, as it resolved down from many of the, settler's testimony from, the location of the old fences, the property lines. There were still some of the old wooden fences between property out here on the east side of Moscow.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And of course, there was a divergence from the true line. What would be the true right angles from, the north and south line? Well, the case was tried out and the judge still decided that the accurate survey must cover that. It was to. They had the lines when properly drawn. The true north and south line.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: That that was the only, that was the true line that had been established. All the descriptions were based on that and that, that would have to govern what the true line would be. But of course, naturally, it would make a considerable, considerable confusion about where these people that from for 50 years had built their, their, all fences and sidewalks and everything based upon the original line that's been laid out on the original survey.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, Mr. Orin took the case, or we took the case, though I didn't. Yeah, I he was his case, but I had worked on it. He took it to the Supreme Court, but the Supreme Court held that when these all lines had been established, established by the earliest pioneers, when they built their houses, built their fences, built their sidewalks on what was the recognized line that had been recognized.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: These lines had been recognized for more than 50 years that the court would not disturb them, and that the lines that had been accepted by everybody from the very beginning would have to go right. And so that's the reason that there was some confusion about the lines here. But there's no question where are the old lines where the surveyors follow those out?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: It does make some difference when you the further you get east. But it would have made tremendous confusion if, if they had followed they, they technically accurate line and the court said no, when you build a whole side of town and people have recognized it all these years, that's the line that governs. And that can be easily followed by an engineer.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Now, the engineers didn't like it very well. But, the fact remains that now that the reason that we, have had some differences out in these and in the east part of town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which side of the, case, was the side that you were?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Oh, I was representing the Stenger, which was Stenger Hill to the old lines. They accepted lines and they sought to establish the technically accurate lines. And, so, of course they had there was no particular hard feeling about it when it was established and the case was returned. And that's the way we stand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you argue, incidentally, that the ramifications of changing. Oh, oh, everything they argued.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Oh, everything was argued. It was, and that was a very carefully tried case. And they there's a tremendous record up in the courthouse now. It will be found up there. Oh. Number of witnesses from the earlier days. You must remember that back in the 20s, most of the early settlers are a large part of them were still alive and could come and testify about where they built their lines and fences and so on.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And it was a very interesting case. It's a long case, but it was, that's why we have had this difference about property lines. Now, next you asked me to say something about prohibition cases. Well, of course I was concerned, as prosecuting attorney only with the, prosecutions under the state prohibition law. And there was a, federal prohibition Act and a state prohibition act.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: The offenses under the State Prohibition Act were they were directed against the manufacture, the sale, or the possession of intoxicating liquor. Now, we had quite a few moonshiners out in the backwoods, in the area of Troy and Bovill and out there, the, of course, the Potlatch Lumber Company would not willingly permit anybody to establish any stills on their forest land, but there are lots of little property owners.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And of course, the usual way that it was handled was that the, moonshiner would bring his liquor to someone who would convey it to the town and the town when they'd convert to the bootlegger, and then the bootlegger would either bottle or sell it by a jug to the ultimate consumer. And, of course, we had quite a few cases.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Most of the, the, the, stills were located. And as I remember, in Clearwater County and down along the snake River, up above Lewiston, where there was wild country, we, had a few cases for manufacture here. The usual defense was that they though it was on the fellow's property that he didn't know it was there, that somebody else must have had the steel and and when they found, cracked corn in his up at his place, he said that was for the purpose of feeding his chickens.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And, of course, often the lead forgotten, added sugar, and we with it. He couldn't explain why there was sugar and or the why it was wet. Sometimes, the, runners, ordinarily had nothing to do with the manufacture. They operated in there by automobile. They'd come into a town and, deliver it to somebody who was the real bootlegger that, I, I that conveyed that that sold it to the, often respected citizen who got it.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We had, quite a lot of cases for sales of liquor around the dances and around where, the lumberjacks were. A lot of these cases were sales to informers who went out and made the buy under the eyes of the sheriff, who was watching. And, there was, we didn't have so many cases for the manufacturer to look through here, but there's quite a lot of them for the sale of liquor.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We had quite a lot of, wine made over in the Potlatch area. There was a, group of Italians who lived there were mostly pretty good people, but they, had made wine. They used wine all their lives. They couldn't see anything wrong with making wine and, making a little money by selling it to thirsty. Native American citizens.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: There was a law, in addition that provided that when a man was more than twice he'd been convicted twice. The third time he could be charged with being a persistent violator of the state prohibition. And that was a penitentiary offense. And we as I remember it, there was only one man that we convicted of being a position violator of all the time.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Usually they got out of the business before they got a chance to go into the penitentiary. But jail sentences are handed out pretty regularly for, for, the bootlegging and, selling was a mere purchaser was charged with possession. Very seldom was he given a jail sentence, given a substantial fine for having liquor in his possession.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I can remember one of the interesting defenses or one of the interesting, cases that arose about a man that had had, bought some liquor. He was charged with liquor in his possession, but before that, he was arrested for the possession of the liquor. And while they were busy with it, the rest in the bootlegger, he drank the liquor, and he was charged with the possession of liquor.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But the court rightly held that when he swallowed the liquor, it became part of his body. And we didn't have any evidence. Now I think that's all I'll say about the prohibition cases, of course, was repealed. The difficulty was that gradually the drinking became the, the, the, the accepted thing in society. And, I know it reached the stage when I was prosecuting attorney, when I'd be invited out to a dinner.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I didn't drink and never took any illegal liquor. But, I simply would notify the hostess that I, we would be half or three quarters of an hour late. And, it was well understood that I would not permit no liquor could be served in my presence. My wife would just arrive after the other. It'd because it got to be a common thing to serve liquor.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But I can say that I, it was never in my presence because it was against the law, and I couldn't understand prosecuting some poor lumberjack for taking a drink and then letting people get away. Substantial citizens get away with it. So I just preferred to have nothing to do with it. Now, to go back, did you ask someone to ask you questions about the probe?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes. There's, one question that I was curious about. And that you broached a little bit when our first talking that was about, the way that the prosecution handled the prohibition cases in general. You told me that you had, a rather set, argument that you gave. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Yes. I will say that to first that in later County, I'm quite sure we had the best record for the enforcement of the prohibition laws of any county in the state. You can understand that up around Wallace and Kellogg and down at Lewiston, they read the ordinary citizen began, a lot of them at least regarded as an infringement upon his rights.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And there was nothing morally wrong about it. But here in later our county, we were quite successful because of the location of the university. And at that time, this was the only state institution and in trying these cases in my argument to the jury and then my question for the jury in my opening statement, I always pointed out that here in Lake, our county, we had a responsibility to the citizens of Idaho, the fathers and mothers who sent their sons and daughters here to the university.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We had a special responsibility, and that I hope that they would not to convict an innocent man, but to keep that in mind, whatever their views were about the prohibition law, the duty we owed to the fathers and mothers of the throughout the state to keep this, this county free of liquor and away from their sons and daughters.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I mean, it was very effective. They took it much more seriously than the ten did the other, the jurors and many other counties. In fact, I don't know whether I mentioned this, but up in Wallace Kellogg, the, the saloons were a they weren't called saloons. Speakeasies were in operation. In fact, under the federal prohibition law, they indicted the mayor of, the, Wallace, the county commissioners, the sheriff, and most of the county officials for conspiracy to violate the National Prohibition Act because they condoned all this.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And I wasn't anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For it was their usual length of sentence.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, first here, well, here, in this county, if the fellow was commercially engaged in, in selling or manufacturing here, they always got a jail sentence. Judge Diedrich, who is the federal district judge here? He always gave the commercial violator a jail sentence. In fact, the congestion got so bad in the back in the in New York City that they, transferred, Judge Diedrich back there for the to help get rid of the backlog.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And they said that when some of these violators were brought in and he sentenced in the jail, like, fade away, they never had such a thing because in many of the cities, they were just given a fine, which would amount to a good sized license fee. And went right on again. But here, and particularly in this county, I don't know about Judge Diedrich and other counties, but, if they are in the commercial business of selling or manufacturing liquors, they got a jail sentence and a good jail sentence.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Pat Malone involved in many of these cases?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: No, not too many. Pat was up there with the lumberjacks. He had to get along with him. He was quite popular. And, well, like the jolly old Irishman. Oh, sure, he had a lot of smaller cases, but we were not so concerned, about the individual lumberjack. Sure, if he was picked up, he'd get a fine if he had some.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Like any possession, we were concerned with a bootlegger that was disposing of it, and he was a pretty educated or old pack. Everybody knew him so well. If he's anywhere in the offing, they've never had any liquor around. But we had a little trouble with any place where they had a speakeasy. There wasn't any such thing much in Boonville.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Oh, once or twice there was some liquor disposed of around, some restaurant or something living there. But. And that. No, not around the restaurant as much as around. Oh, some fellow that operated, places for cigars and dance halls and things of that kind. But it wasn't a severe case up there in old Pat was hard on the trail, but he just, we didn't have any real big cases that he was involved in.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: A very interesting old Irishman that I always look back with fondness, as I do to Albert. Strong, strong. Who was the marshal down at Troy now? We had quite a few offenses down at Troy. There was always a Saturday night dance down there. And there is always a liquor get, a little liquor disposed of. Strom was a fine, honorable town marshal.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Did a fine job. A lot of things. Besides just being town marshal. He had to look after the the waterworks and collecting the water bills. And strong is, was a fine man. And, I look back with pleasure talk to him and also to the constable over at Potlatch. Now, the constable was. And an Englishman by the name of Harry Hale.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: M initials Gleave. Geo a v e now he was appointed constable and townsite superintendent by the Potlatch Lumber Company. Because, you see, they had to have somebody in charge of all the buildings in the town and the town buildings there, because, you see, Potlatch was built entirely by the Potlatch Lumber Company to house their workers and their various buildings.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: There was and there was a gymnasium and a theater and so on. They were all built by the Potlatch Lumber Company. The store at that time was run by a subsidiary of the Potlatch Lumber Company. And, in that store, for instance, a fella could draw checks and advance slips on his pay if he ran out of money before, he, before the end of the month.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And these this scrip could be either cash at the bank or given to the Potlatch Mercantile to buy goods for his family. If he had run out of money, then that he'd be charged again when his paycheck came in at the end of the month. Well, now, Gleave had charge of all these houses and seeing that they were prepared, he didn't do the carpenter work himself.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But he was the general superintendent of this whole establishment that was owned by the by the potlatch. Now, true, once in a while he became involved in some case, but he didn't. He was too busy with his other work. And I'll tell you that Harry and Glebe, now, since deceased, was one of the finest citizens we had here in late County, he did an excellent and efficient job as the townsite superintendent, but he was also a constable besides being the townsite superintendent.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And he was responsible, of course, as constable for maintaining order in the community. But Potlatch itself was a fairly, very law abiding community. You see, the main the major mill was located there. The town itself was, well run under, constable superintend superintendent leaves direction and supervision. Now, there was a little town of Onaway, next to it that wasn't owned by the potlatch.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And there's considered more and more difficulty and, trouble about selling liquor and, various crimes, not serious ones out in Onaway, which wasn't under the supervision of Gilley. And he had nothing to do with that. That was a sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In other words, in the during the period of prohibition, Potlatch was really for the kind of town being a lumber town was really quite, a clean water town.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: They had a pool hall there. True. But it was very, everything was clean. The company insisted on not only the sure the in the individual lumberjack out in the woods. That was a different situation because a large part of them were single men. But in Potlatch, most of the, mill workers were married men who rented homes there.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: From the potlatch, it was a very substantial community. There was a bank there. That was all. The stock was owned by the Potlatch Lumber Company. And, it was, there we had little difficulty about Potlatch itself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or there's one more question I think I have about prohibition, and that is, the way that that the cases were or broken and the, the, offenders were, were brought in. Did the did you as prosecuting attorney have much involvement with that part of the, of the the business, or was were you involved only after the the case was broken and how were the cases broken generally.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they broken by informers or by the, the sheriff's office or, or that just general.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, generally I'd say that that, I had little to do with the actual apprehension of bootleggers and so on. My job was prosecution, and I went out. My trips out through the county were usually involved more serious offenses, because I had left word with the sheriff when there was any serious offense apparent to call me at once when he heard of it, and take me along with him so that if you know, if it was a manslaughter case, a bad assault case, or a robbery, burglary or anything of this kind, I was there with him, right on the ground where I could talk to the witnesses, where he.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: He had made an arrest. I could I could talk to the accused. And and it was a great help. I if I do say it, I had a very good record for, for, convictions because I was there, right on the scene right afterwards with the sheriff and, and we quickly made up for the mind whether the man was guilty and next, whether we had a case against, you know, to the apprehension of these fellows.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: There were bootleggers. Yes. We had there were some farmers hired occasionally somebody that would be a stranger, maybe somebody from another part of county wouldn't be well known who would try to make buys, as we call it. And they they'd try to make the buy where the sheriff would be watching. And then if, you know, in a small county like this, if somebody was in the liquor business locally, the word got around and you'd get to the sheriff's office and remember that there were plenty of good some citizens strongly in favor of the prohibition Act, who would tip the sheriff off if there was somebody.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: There was word around that it fellow was manufacturing liquor. And usually if the sheriff would if there was report to manufacture, he'd, quietly slip out in the area, perhaps locate the steel back in the woods if nobody was there, conceal himself. And when the fellow came to attend these, still he'd be picked up. But, the runners that came in from outside, they were seldom caught because they were strangers who ran, would run a car in here, they'd have some, be in touch with somebody locally who wanted the liquor.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: They meet to have some place they meet outside of town, and it always be at night. They'd meet at some, road corner at a certain time, and the next time it'd be changed to some other time, it was pretty difficult to to locate and apprehend them. And of course, when, lumber some lumberjack bought a bottle and got drunk and was making a disturbance, was picked up and had a bottle on him, why, of course, that was an easy thing to, to charge him and convict him.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And they'd usually plead guilty and not get usually not be given a very severe fine. If we were convinced that he had nothing to do with the financial, gain in the thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When this law was repealed, were you as, prosecuting attorney, sort of glad to see it go and not have to deal with these kinds of cases?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, yes, I would say I was, I, I can't remember whether I when I went out as a prosecuting attorney, I think I went out in 1934 and it was over with. But then but of course. Oh, well, I certainly was glad to get at some of these fellows that were selling rotten liquor that had blind people and so on.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Besides, it was the law, and it was for a worthy purpose, and it was on the books, and it was my job to enforce it. And I did the very best I could.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay. I'd like to ask you about a case that that you mentioned just when we stopped last time. That was the the cattle rustling case involving Galloway brothers.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, that case, my I wish I had could look over the file and refresh my memory, but the Galloway brothers ran cattle down on the other side of Kennedy. Some of their cattle were stolen. I can't even remember the name of the defendant. And, And there were several circumstances that made them think that he was guilty of it, and I don't remember the details.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But anyway, they filed a complaint. We had a they had this man arrested. He was brought in here. He was charged with the offense. I don't remember whether he insisted on having a preliminary hearing before the probate judge, who was then the magistrate. I really don't remember, but because he had a right to just waive that, and he was bound over to the district court.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I filed an information against him for grand larceny, for stealing the cattle, and I don't remember all the details in connection with the case or just how much evidence they had against him. But when the case came up for trial, that is the court term was coming up. I made up my mind that we didn't have a sufficient case to convict him, and I thought it would be a bad thing to bring him in here.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And if we once impaneled a jury and he was acquitted, he was gone for good. He couldn't be prosecuted again. So, though my memory now, what evidence we had is not very strong. I present the motion to District Judge Steele to dismiss the case. The dismissal had no effect on another prosecution. And upon the grounds that I didn't consider it, the evidence was sufficient to sustain a conviction.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, the the judge on my motion dismissed the case, and the Galloway boys were certainly pretty hot about it, and I couldn't blame them. I was convinced of the fellows guilt, but I just thought we didn't have the case. And I, we hadn't had tough time enough to run it down as fully as I thought we should.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: So I dismissed the case, and, and, they were very much dissatisfied with it. And, I had a lot of criticism from people down there for doing it, but I did what I thought was the best thing. Well, time went on, and we worked on the case further, and the sheriff worked on the case, and I offered to and I said to the Galloway boys, now, look, I think the fella's guilty, too.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Now you try to give me some more evidence and the sheriff will work on it. And let's get in touch with the sheriff down at Clearwater County, because it was on the border of Clearwater County. No, I don't remember the details. It's been so long ago. But I do remember that we finally built up quite a case. The next thing was that at this fellow that we let go and the case had been dismissed.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Dismissed. Went up Spokane. And lo and behold, I read in the paper that he had been convicted of chicken stealing over there in, some place out of Spokane. Well, now, theft the chickens had been made a felony by the Washington statute. It was a felony, a penitentiary offense to steal chickens. They'd had so much trouble up there.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: So along before the next term of court came up, I had got Galloway doing in and had him swear to another complaint. And then we tried the case. Well, as a result of careful work on the part of our sheriff and of others and the work that the Galloway's done, we first our first witness and strongest witness against him was, the the butcher of the Elk River.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We finally run down. Frederick disposed of some of the cattle. Carl Jockheck. J... e c t I think that's how he spelled his name. Was the butcher up there. And he was also a leading citizen of Elk River. And if they had a a town organization, I think he was the head of it, a very fine, upright citizen, a very pleasant fellow.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, we he after the, original arrest of, not after the original arrest, but after the case had been dismissed. Know running it down. We found it. Carl Jockheck had bought some some cattle from the fellow that we accused, and fortunately, he had retained the hides and got job. Cat got on the stand and told about that day.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: A year or so after the alleged theft, that, the defendant came to him and he bought the cattle and paid him for him. And 1 or 2 of the hides had the Galloway's brand on it. And, we had the hides here with college jock to identify them. And, jock identified the accused as being the man that he purchased the Galloway cattle from.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: In addition to that, and we had some other evidence as other original evidence. They'd had two bodies being in the presence and down in the area where these were these horses and so on. Some of the original evidence we had of Galloway, the defendant took the stand in his own defense. And that's just what I'd been waiting for.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: So when he took the stand and after he'd testified that he'd had nothing to do it, that Carl Jockheck was mistaken. It wasn't him and really had nothing to do with it was a mistaken identity that he had, but nothing to do with the case. And that Carl Jockheck was certainly mistaken, and he had a witness or two to show that he was someplace else at the time, an alibi that he couldn't have possibly been there, and that, I, he then after his examination is witnesses over then he is then, and he'd given his testimony, then it was time for cross-examination.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And then, of course, then I simply said to him, Mr. Jockheck, not Mr. Jockheck. It must be that I said to the accused, have you ever been convicted of a felony? And if he wasn't, didn't want to answer a judge? I insisted that he answered and the judge said, yes, that's a proper question. To me to, test his credibility because under the law, conviction of a felony and, yes, he is an impeachment of a man of integrity.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And it's a proper way to impeach this testimony. So the accused and yes, I was I said, where was that? He said, in Spokane, Washington. And, I said, of course I could do. As to any questions, I had the certified copy from Spokane of the judgment of conviction, obviously, in the Superior Court there and of the sentence give it to him.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And reading for that, I asked him all these various questions, which he admitted, and then following that, I submitted the, judgment of the Superior Court of Spokane County, of his conviction of a grand of grand larceny. That was the very thing that we charged him with here. I we didn't go. What it was. It was grand larceny.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: It was the conviction. Well, we had built up a strong case, particularly with Carl Jockheck's testimony. And then this was the clincher. And the judge will answer my question. Request instructed the jury that one of the, ways to impeach the, credibility was witness either was by proof of contrary statements made at some other time or his, or a proof that he had been convicted of a felony and that they had a right to consider that in going out to consider the story and the credibility of the defendant and the accusing, the jury convicted him in a hurry.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: He was sentenced to penitentiary. But I have always had a great respect for the Galloway boys. And I don't blame you for being mad or angry at me, but just the same, I think we would have lost that first case, and particularly that conviction of grand larceny later in Spokane was the clincher that did the business with the jury.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That seems like very careful judgment on your part to hold it.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I don't know about that place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One thing I'd like to ask you about, and I was thinking about when you talked about the, US District court meeting in Moscow. It occurred to me that that meant that the Indian cases would be tried in the district court there, too. Is that.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Correct? That's right. All the Indian cases from the reservation all tried here. And of course they were. There's tried a lot of cases, federal cases for selling liquor to Indians. And of course, occasionally there is some serious crime committed or accused who was an Indian and against an Indian, and it was an offense on the reservation. So those cases were tried here.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We always had a few Indian cases. Every time a court.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there were there any particular particularly?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: No, I I'll tell you, I don't really think there was any law. I'm sure there were some murders and on like that, but I was busy with my county prosecutor and of course I was practicing law. Was that time also because, you see, my salary is only $1,500 a year, but turned. But I, I, carefully avoided any cases in which there were could possibly be any conflict of interest.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And my cases were all between individual citizens and no connection with the county. And I had nothing to do with any case where the county could possibly be involved.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you ever involved in, murder or manslaughter cases?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Oh, I was involved in several manslaughter cases. I'm not sure about murder cases. I. I was involved in a number of manslaughter cases. I don't remember any murder cases. I've defended manslaughter cases and assault with a deadly weapon cases. Both tried them and and defended them after I got out because I did do some criminal work. But I didn't get into much criminal work.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Unless it was some very important case or where I had some sympathy for the accused. And I knew his family, and I was personally convinced of his innocence. I tried some fairly important cases that way, and I did a lot of work in trying automobile accident cases, bank cases, insurance cases, railroad cases. I was attorney for the, union general attorney here for the Union Pacific, for the Great Northern.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And I tried cases both for and against the Great Northern Army and the Northern Pacific Railway. I wouldn't take an appointment. They wanted me to be their local attorney, the Northern Pacific. But I didn't want to do it. I tried one case against the Northern Pacific for the, death of a 12 year old boy out on the Northern Pacific Railway, on the railway that goes out toward, toward the cemetery out there.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: That case I brought in the federal district court, because it involved the, Great Northern or the Northern Pacific Railway, which was not an Idaho corporation, was a diversity of citizenship. I won that case, and I wanted in a peculiar way, I had it was admitted the train was going about 25 miles an hour at the time it struck.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: This used a very moderate speed, but I dug up an old, city ordinance passed in 1904 that provided that no train should go faster than 12 miles an hour within the city limits of Moscow. And, the federal district judge, when I we got all the facts in and, you know, our case and, the, and the, I had submitted my property, authenticated copy of the ordinance of the City of Moscow through the city clerk, and it was still in full force, in effect.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And since I had shown that it was in within the city limits that the train going to, if they admitted it was going 20 or 25 miles an hour, there was nobody lived out that way, but it was still in the city limits. The court held that that proved negligence per se, and that it was up to the jury to determine, what the, the amount of the, the verdict for the parents.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And we got a handsome recovery, the great and our the Northern Pacific that appealed the case to the Circuit Court of Appeals. But before we went down to argue that, San Francisco, the, the railroad finally settled in a satisfactory way. And, we cut down our verdict some, the case was originally brought to me by Bill Fowler, who was also practicing here, but he had been trying many cases to find follow.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And, he brought the case to me, but I tried the case, and and, but Fowler was a very fine fellow who later gave up the practice law and, but. Well, he lived here for some time, and then, was a reserve officer got in the was called to duty with the Civilian Conservation Corps two years later and stayed in the service and served all through World War two and then later died short.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well before he was released from the army after World War two. But he did become a premier.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do work for a Potlatch Lumber Company?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Oh, I tried cases against him. And for, I tried very well. And one very important case against them. I examined and a lot of abstracts for the, I had work for the potlatch when I was in college, and. And Mr. Laird, who was the, in charge of the whole operation here, when I early in my practice was there by left timber, he brought a lot of abstracts to me to be examined and, very helpful to me in my early days.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: They were very friendly to me. After, Laird left, I. I was instrumental in a case brought against Charles Hogan, who was then the constable there. It had gone and for, firing a gas pistol and a fellow that was being arrested for disorderly conduct. And they put out that damages. I we won that case. I in the end, we won that case in the state court.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But it was finally overturned on appeal in the participating state Supreme court after I'd gone away and was it was in Africa, 12,000 miles away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you worked on the abstracts for them, were these the purchases of the of Timberlake.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Upon or you see that there was landowner had been homesteaded and out here that they were buying this land and in those days there were no title companies operating here, and there was an individual abstract made up for each, piece of land. And it was examined to determine whether the title was clear before they purchased it. Now, you see, we have this title insurance that the company checks the titles from the records, and then they insured the purchaser against any loss by reason of that title.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But we didn't have those then. And that examination of abstracts was all a thing of the past. That's done by the abstract.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what about this story that you mentioned about the way that the mill came to Potlatch instead of to Moscow? There was controversy about that or.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Oh, well, I it's too bad to go into it. It was before my time very briefly. And they're there books that tell about this and there's some interesting books that tell about very briefly it was this, the Potlatch Company bought up the mill at Colfax earlier, or somebody bought up the mill number of mill down Colfax. I mean, they bought up the mill of pollution.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: There's a mill operating at Colfax because the South Palouse was heavily timbered in the early days. Clear down to cool facts. They used to run logs, down to cool facts. Then they ran them down to to Palouse. The, the potlatch got those mills, and they built the mill over there, and they wanted to build them and build a mill for the great mass of timber between pollution that was heavy timber between that and the Montana line.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Bill dairy was sent out by the Potlatch Lumber Company, a main corporation that had been operating in Michigan and Wisconsin had cut that all off that commercial timber off there and wanted to move out here that had acquired large tracts of land here. They sent dairy out here to select a site for the mill. He came where were to Moscow.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Moscow. Martin was then heavily timber diverged about 99 or 98. Moscow. Interests, of course, wanted the mill to be located here. They were going to build a logging railroad that was an Idaho and Montana railroad, and they wanted it built from here. But, but, dairy came out and came out and looked things over and they, looked over a possible site on Come around Gulch or someplace here.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: He looked into the matter of acquiring a right away from here up to their timber, up in the dairy Bovill area, up there. But some of the property owners here or an anxious to make a killin, and they certainly held up the price. And so dairy deer. He looked around elsewhere. He finally made a report to his directors.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And as I understand the story, Bill dairy, a good Irish Catholic who later became what they called the bull of the woods, he was the loan superintendent out here, wrote back to his directors something like this. I have looked over a possible site of our mill. I've looked carefully over Moscow, which is a prosperous town and would be a nice location, for the community of South.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But he said, I've also looked up the location on the other side of the mountain, a mountain, and on the south Palouse River. I have come to the conclusion that you can't build a mill unless you've got plenty of water for your mill race and for your mill pond, and in my opinion, there's not enough water in the Moscow area to baptize a Protestant bastard.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: So, I strongly recommend they be able to mill over potash. And that's what they did. And, of course, as it eventually developed, why he would write about it in the the he river, there's ample water. And there he was. Right. There wasn't really sufficient water here. Then he'd run into this trouble about the, the, building the railroad and instead of hooking it on to the Northern Pacific or the Union Pacific here, they hooked it on to the, Northern Pacific over it.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Palouse. And they ran it on their own land, mostly on their own land from there to up to, to, the Milwaukee Road at, but, Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay. What about the depression?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, now I think that's important. Before we get through, Whether there was a worldwide brewing, world, right. Depression that started in the late 20s, there had been a depression after the end of World War One along in the early 20s. There had been quite a depression, and but not a real seriousness. Depression. It you see, the adjusting of industry after World War One, there had been such a dislocation.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: The, the, livestock men and the sheep man, got into a real, depression. There was a lot of changes in manufacturing from manufacturing, war machines and war, supplies and ammunition to go back to peacetime. And so on. That was quite a depression in the early, in the early 20s. But by the later two, later 20 a.m., starting in about 1928 and particularly in 1929, when the big stock market crash came, the whole world was more in a state of disorder and depression.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Hoover was blamed for it. But, he did every means in his power. And ability that was used in the campaign against him. When any of the president Hoover, who had one of the most wonderful records as an administrator and as a public servant, was blamed for it. And, of course, he was voted out in the greatest landslide that we've ever had in a national election, almost.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Maybe there's been some sense, but, certainly when Johnson was elected over, Goldwater and and of course, Nixon had quite, a landslide. But, after Franklin Roosevelt was elected, then the real depression started, and all over the country there was widespread unemployment here. There was some of the, the concerns began to suffer. The stores, some of the stirrers had got a few of them.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I had to quit business, though. Our main stores stayed through it because people couldn't pay their bills. There was widespread unemployment. Fortunately, the partnership, kept up much of its employment, but, so that we weren't hit as bad there as we might have been. But the big thing that hit us here and locally was the price of wheat.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: The price of wheat went down to from 25 to $0.29, a bushel. And, the farmers had had unrivaled prosperity during World War One, and they'd greatly expanded their efforts, bought lots of machinery, built fine homes and remade their barns and everything else. Borrowed money at the bank to do this. And they, they weren't able to pay their bills.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We went down so cheaply, it wouldn't even begin to pay the cost of producing it. Well, the banks had the heavy load down all over the county. The these farmers these here to for prosperous farmers. The price of livestock went down. The wheat price just hit rock bottom. Farmers couldn't pay their bills. They couldn't pay their notes at the bank.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And the banks, were no longer in a liquid condition. And, by 1930, the, Roosevelt was elected in 32. By 1933, when he took office, there had been the crash. The banks all over the country were failing with their loans to industry and so on. When various industry went out of business and so on, and had to close down and, so, the situation here was so bad then to everybody was uncertain and very uneasy.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And, I was then a director and Mr. Orin and I were attorneys for the First National Bank here in Moscow, a local bank. There was the first trust from savings Bank, a local bank, and there was the Moscow State Bank, a local bank. John Hess Hickerson was cashier of the of the First National Bank. Hawk and mail guard was president, and William Cahill was the was the cashier at the first Crescent Savings Bank.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Robert Wood, he was president of the Moscow State Bank. Harry Witter was cashier. And all these were fine men, active public citizens, male guard had had been or was later mayor of Moscow. Harry Whittier and Robert Whittier were active in the community. Yes, Catherine was much respected that later ran for Congress 10 or 15 years after the depression.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But there was uncertainty everywhere. And we are all worried about the banks, the banks. We knew that the banks in these small towns were having trouble. There are banks that, both Bill and the dairy at, Juliana at, Genesee, besides these banks here in Moscow. Well, one night along about midnight, I got a call from Hackett's on dime.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: The cashier of our bank asking me to come down to the to the, bank at once because they're going to have a meeting of the directors, and I didn't know what was up, but I was worried, and I went down. We found the directors who were there. There was, I remember that our directors. I remember where Tim Sullivan, the Ford ran the Ford agency.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: There was, Max Griffith, who ran the, credit bureau here and was an attorney. There was, Tom ran up at Grange. Bill was a director, but he wasn't able to be at the meeting. He was an old pioneer. Jacko Thorne, myself and C.J. Arland. And when we got down there, Jacobson told us that he just had a call from Spokane that the old National Bank of Spokane was not going to reopen the next morning.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, that was a catastrophe for us because, like most banks, we kept a liquid sum of money to take care of the regular business, cash on hand to take regular business depositors. There. We could handle any ordinary business, and we had quite a cushion besides that. But we had been very careful in our loans and had quite a substantial sum of money.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: On deposit at the Old National Bank. The old National Bank had an interest in stock in our bank, and we headed up there extra money for, emergency. And, it was quite a custom for banks to let this money that was, surplus that wasn't being used to get interest on that. And, but we had our money up there and, he said we if when this gets in the paper tomorrow morning when it comes out, we're going to have a run on the bank.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We're going to have a run on all the banks here in town because people will be worried. Here's the big bank in Spokane goes blank. Everybody's going to run into town in to all the banks here and, demand their money. And of course, no bank can just have a hall of cash on hand to meet every depositors.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Account. We can meet the normal amount and have some extra to be sure if there's a partial run. But if everybody come in to manage the money, it's going to put every bank out of business here in town. So we talked it over, and I sat down and drew a proclamation and took it home. For instance, who was the mayor of Moscow?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Woke him up the they got him to sign the proclamation. It was a proclamation declaring a legal holiday in Moscow for the next day. He signed it and went off home. Finally got to bed, got down to the office bank next day, and here the banks in town had a proclamation up the window. Legal holiday today declared by, by order of the mayor.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And these people are milling around. Of course, they read about this and they were all excited and, and to come in to get their money and, of course, Melgar guard, did a fine job. He was well respected by all the Scandinavian pop Arizona population. He was out in front of his bank mail guard was never, Hickerson was down there.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: They, people came up, milled around, and they explained that the bank was in good shape if everybody ran in and demanded the money, of course they're going to have trouble. And, you know, Moscow's a pretty good place. They were kind of finally convinced by these men that the arresting and, and, and, demanded their money, that everybody was going to lose out.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But unfortunately, the Moscow State Bank was in such bad state, and they had so much invested in foreign loans that couldn't be paid for and landed, depreciated so much in value that they had to close and close permanently. Now by that, two days later, the President Roosevelt declared a national bank holiday so that staved off that. And then there were various acts passed, that enabled the banks to establish different kinds of accounts and so on.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And as a result of the substantial citizens in Moscow, the first trust bank in the Moscow and the First National Bank weathered the storm. And, we had some difficulty about the money that was in the old National Bank. But we eventually got that, though we were authorized to open reopen shortly before we got it. The after careful examination of our books, we were permitted to open, kind of a special account where they couldn't a draw on their own for a while and only a part of it.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: But we reopened. But the Moscow State Bank was unable to, whether it was closed and all the other banks and the county closed, all of them were liquidated. And, the real difficulty was the low price we did the way farmland went down and, we eventually came out of it. One of the interesting developments was the passage by the Congress to the National Recovery Act, and we established what was called the NRA.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Have you got plenty of room there to get this in?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I think I'd like to start another. We'll start from the beginning.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: To compare the way people live now when they think they're hard up now. Why do an old timer. They're lived in unparalleled, munificence the way people live now. And when I hear some fellow kicking about how hard he up up here is, how terrible it is and how price, how price, high price food is. And this fella has 1 or 2 colored televisions and 1 or 2 cars and a radio, and he has an electric washing machine and, and an electric dryer and all this equipment, you see, people, you just have to adjust yourself to what you can live on.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And those people didn't enjoy all the convenience that we think we have to have now. And of course, there wasn't then to that extent, so much dealing in credit and buying everything own credit and buying all their furniture on credit. And in those days, a man didn't get married unless he had a job, that he would support a wife.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Now they get married whether they have a job or not, and the women work until a few women were working. Then sure, they had to tighten their belt. Sure, people cooked at home, that thing that, then all the grocery stores where that was before the chain stores and before these closed so-called supermarkets. Then you went to the grocery store and, and you bought flour by the sack, but you, everything else you was a cheese was in a great big wheel.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: You cut off what you wanted. You went to the butcher, and you wanted to roast any whole the quarter out and slammed it on the, slammed it on the, block and cut off what you wanted. And if you wanted a steak, he cut it off and then wrapped it up in butcher paper. There. There was none of this.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: All this individual packaging of every little thing. And, things were sold in bulk, largely. And, you see, all that cost money, cost labor to do all that. And of course, people insist they kick about the high. There was all the high price of all this prepared and frozen food and everything. We didn't have that then. Food was cheaper because it wasn't all that labor involved.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Now, of course, people demanded when this came on. Now, they in the old style grocery store has gone out of business. Everything has to be packed in cellophane, has to be cut and priced and weighed and labeled and and it has to be so that the housewife can take it home and put it in the in the oven and cook it right as it is.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: There is people live much simpler there, and you had to be a good cook. You had to take and combine and use the course. There were, bakeries, but there was lots of baking done at home. And, and but the main thing is about these supermarkets, people have to pay for all this labor that is expended.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And the supermarket doesn't have it. They just, well, go out of business. People will put up with it. But if we got into another depression, they could learn that you can live a lot cheaper. You never have to have all these things. And those people did do that and we didn't have any real want here. People were very good about helping each other and, their grew gardens and, people got along and then did it and did.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And I just wonder, these people have got to the state that they've got to have everything prepared before that there's just so convenient. But they have to pay for it. And that's one of the difficulties. I know that they just live so infinitely better and have so many more conveniences. Those people have them back in the early 30s.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you talk about the depression that followed World War one, the minor depression and the problems nationally, other two things that made me wonder. First was that operating here and secondly, they talk about prosperity in the 20s. There was a lot of prosperity. Oh, there we have that here, too. Is there a lot of factories?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Yes. And not quite as great here. Oh, yes, I'd say so. We eventually there was a slight depression due to the dislocation of things. And now about from two or 3 or 4 years after World War one, it ended in 1918. Then we developed an unparalleled prosperity that led to the use to the stock market crash in 1929, when everybody was investing money in manufacturing things.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: People were selling land at elevated prices. They were buying land at high prices. There was tremendous building going on and everything. They overbuilt, they, overextended in the construction of plants and particularly in Florida, overbuilt in California. They overbuilt. The land was too high priced. Our farmland here was too high price. There was a period, but the crash came in 29 and then gradually deepened into a continuing depression that things kept getting worse and worse until about 1933.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: It was the worst. And then it remained at a kind of a bad level up to look until the European war started. And 38 or 39.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's one point on the on the NRA that I and that I wasn't entirely clear on as far as the local group went, is is were you saying that that your main role of the local group was to encourage.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: All interior comply voluntary compliance? It was based on voluntary compliance? We encourage people for the general good to comply with these codes that had been promulgated. We didn't prosecute anybody here. We just relied on the patriotic, desire of employers and employees and of consumers to live up to their price scale and to live up to the hours of labor and to and things of that kind.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We didn't prosecute anybody. We it was a entirely voluntary concern in which the community shared in generally.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And and when you talked about the, the depression, catching the farmers up short here as far as their expansion goes, is that that's just part of the the general prosperity of the 20s that that farmers and people in the county also were believed that the times were maybe more prosperous than they were.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And they were. Yes, they were, and prices were elevated and so on. They bought lots of machinery and they did lots of improving and but just a little too strong. And that's what brought on the 1929 depression. But really, it was the farm situation here that that hit us the hardest. And that's what broke the banks. Now, look, I didn't want to go on very much further.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And my wife sitting out there waiting, waiting for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Waiting.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We had to cut that out. Okay, well, now I'll tell you. Now, you've asked me about my service in the state Senate. Well, in the late 40s, I was elected to the state Senate. I went down to, Boise. My wife went with me, and we had two small children. I went down there first located in an apartment, decided the best thing to do would be to bring the family down.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And I found that I committed my children in school. So, we packed my wife, packed our regular dishes and a few other things that we'd need in this apartment. And, we rented an apartment for the session. I went down and, we entered the children in school. We had a very interesting time. The. At that time, of course, the the big interest was the appropriation for the university.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And that's always true with somebody here from later County. I had a very interesting time. There was lots of social functions. Chase Clark was the governor. He was a lawyer and an old friend of mine. And in fact, when my son had his birthday, Governor Clark invited him into his office and had his picture taken.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: It was in the Boise paper with my son, Tim, sitting in the governor's chair on his birthday. Well, of course, there are plenty of things that came up in the session. I think that probably the most worthwhile thing I did there during that time, from my standpoint, was that, I was unable to introduce and get through the legislature.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: That bill, sponsored by the Bar Association, which recognized the right of the courts to fix their own procedure, in other words, they this was verified by statute that the courts would fix the procedure. The Congress, I mean, the, legislature would fix all the criminal laws and the basic civil laws. But the procedure that was to be followed in the courts, it was recognized by statutes that the court could make the rules for handling the procedure of the and trying the cases in the courts.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And that was passed. It's in our federal or in our state statute. No. And I think I can claim the major credit for getting it through the, through the legislature. And it's a basic right that, we were one of the early states that got such a law in effect. And it's continue to operate effectively, and it's been followed by a number of other states in the Union.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Now, another bill that I was interested in, was the bill to provide judges for, I mean, provide, retirement for state judges at all levels. And this was worked out on a basis where the judges would contribute part of it. And the, state would contribute part of it that finally passed the legislature.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And we do now, there's been some additions and changes in the law, but we did work out a, a retirement statute for our Supreme Court and, and our district court judges. Oh. There are a number of other matters affecting agriculture, forestry and so on. That I had a part in, if I do say it, I did play a prominent part in the, in the state legislature, and Mrs. Goff proved very popular.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We were active socially. She was an attractive blond. She had been a, head of the, she had been a teacher in the Boise School for three years before, sometime before we were married, she had a wide acquaintance there. She'd been the acting head of the women's physical education department here at the university.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Although in those days, there wasn't a competitive sports for women. And, their principal job was teaching folk dancing and putting on the feet and things of that kind, because there wasn't the a number of competitive sports. And then, of course, there was simply physical education. And but it wasn't a competitive sports thing. And she was an accomplished dancer and knew all these dances and so on.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: She was very popular socially in Boise and the Boise State School and its society. Page. At the end of the session, carried a large picture, on in the society page, with an article, our favorite legislative wife. And, they went all through her, our stay in the socialites of Boise at the time. And enumerated why she was the favorite legislative wife of that session.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And she was extremely helpful to me. And, was so. And later I went to Congress. I came home from the legislature, later was called active duty for a short period with the third Division, regular Army Division headquarters and, Fort Lewis, to which I was assigned, and then came back and then later in, August of 1941, I was called to duty in the Army and went to Washington, DC from defense.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I was sent overseas a month after Pearl Harbor to the British Eighth Army in the Middle East, with headquarters at Cairo. I had some adventures getting there where there was a small group, U.S. military, North African Mission, which were with the British British forces. I was there in the spring of 1941, I see in 42, when Rommel made his great drive for Cairo.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I was in Cairo when he was outside, was there when, we heard over the radio, radio that Mussolini was coming over, flying over from Italy to take part in the triumphal entry to Cairo. But it didn't work out that way. I was with the British forces in some of the bombing and out in the Western Desert, but Rommel was finally turned back.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I was there, and then down in, Italian East Africa, over in the rest of the Middle East, over in Iran, in Iraq, in the Holy Land, in that area, through the Middle East, down on the Red sea. And it came home after, Rondo and Rumbold had been been, had his force had been surrendered and, and and, North African, it was all over.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Of course, I served after that in the United States. I served in Europe, principally with the, Judge Advocate, in the Judge Advocate General of the Army, where I was assistant chief of the International Law Division. Then I was sent over to Europe and matters over these punishment of war crimes. I later served in, in Africa.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I mean, later served in the Pacific and was on general MacArthur's personal staff in the occupation of Japan. I came back as assistant, as deputy director of the U.S War Crimes Office. I joined Army and Navy officers from France went into the undersecretary as War Office. Chair of War Office, as a member of a an Army Clemency Board.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And from that I had been nominated for Congress while I was still there. There was a provision that permitted that, and I'd been nominated while I was still in the Army. I, in the primary came home in the 1st September 1945, got into campaign, which elected, went to Congress as a freshman congressman, defeating a big comp denier.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: White, who had had 13 terms as a Democrat. This was called a Democrat district. I defeated him by only about 2000 votes. It was a close race, and it was a great surprise when when I was elected, and because first I had little chance to campaign and next this was considered a settled Democratic district. I went to the Congress, took my wife and family with me.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We, left here before Christmas. In fact, we took the train because the train was the way to travel there. It was in the winter time. Took the kids with us after Christmas on the train, went back to Washington. I had my deal. I had selected as my office secretary, the secretary to the International Law Division department in the Army, where I had been the assistant chief, a very competent woman.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I selected her as my secretary, congressional secretary in advance. And one would be the name of Margaret Brown, widow. And, and she, had rented a house for us there in Washington, but we stayed at first with an old friend from Idaho who was a graduate in the university. And, who was then the commandant of that out at the Army War College.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We stayed there the first night, and we went on out to our home. If I do say it myself, I got along pretty well in the Congress. We formed an organization of all the first year Republican members of Congress. And I from out here in the comparatively. Sparsely populated, far out western state of Idaho, was elected president of it of the organization.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And, Richard Nixon, by the way, from California, was elected secretary. And, we knew each other well, we're good friends. Florence was good friends of Mrs. Nixon, but, that was an organization of the freshman Republican members of the Congress. We had regular meetings every two weeks, and we usually had somebody, of outstanding national figure as the speaker.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I remember we had Allen Dulles as a speaker at one time. We had, Herbert Hoover another time, I met all these people personally. We had a number of other prominent people. I was the presiding officer and conducted the meetings. It was a very interesting organization. Thereafter, in the session, I was named to the Agriculture Committee on Agriculture, which was very important here because with our farming and of course, agriculture, the Department of Agriculture also has forestry and forestry, all came under the report under the Committee on Agriculture.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And while I was on this committee, we had had a lot of trouble here about the infestation of the tussock north and our forests out here. And I introduced and pushed through, I can say, and we had lots of help, but I had reduced the department bill on that was called the National Forest Pest Control Act, which provided for the, Department of Agriculture to handle the spraying of, of, infested land, whether it was tussock moth or whatever it was.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And that's the act that authorized that and that I can claim that I was the one who got started, you know, our Committee on Agriculture, we got that bill through. And another bill that I introduced was the what I call the Air Supremacy Act. I took the position that the most important thing for national defense, and I was a very strong one.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: It was to build up our Air Force and the bill that I introduced, it had been proposed that we spend $2 billion to build up an air fleet, and my bill would appropriated $3 billion more to make it the make, the United States Air Force, the strongest short period of any in the world, infinitely superior to any in the world, because I took the position that, no foreign enemy could, we were not subject.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: We had already had a strongest fleet, nowhere of that. There's no foreign enemy could, could attack us at sea. That what we had to worry about was a foreign attack through the air. And I opposed the, the, universal military service because I said the emphasis was on the wrong place. It wasn't men we needed that.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Our, tag in this that, was only likely antagonists. Then after World War two was the communist state of Russia, and it was hopeless to match them in, in manpower that we would never invade Russia, that both Napoleon and history and, and Hitler tried to do that, and that what we needed was airpower and that we would never, be attacked except by air and, that if we had the strongest air force in the world, that would ensure that we'd never have to use it.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Well, my bill didn't pass in that for. But it has the result of that. And, the, the, armed forces, I mean, the, the, committee and the, the in committee and in Congress, on, has that still operating?

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Behind. Not to push through my bill called the Air Printed Privacy Act as such, but they enormously increased the appropriation for the Air Force and the building of planes. And, it's a combination of the Armed Services Committee and the Appropriations Committee. They did exactly what I, recommended in this separate bill, I'd introduced. So I can claim that I had something to do.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I wasn't an airman, but I felt so strongly that we should build up. Our Air force is the surest defense against any foreign attack. And that's what they did. And we did develop this tremendous bomber fleet, as the result of the action of the Congress in 1946. Now, in addition to this organization of the freshman Young Republicans, the speaker of the House, Joe Martin, decided that we should have a Republican steering committee.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: This would be composed of leading members of the, of the Republican Party who would assist the speaker in pushing important legislation in, in the Congress. And I, had the good fortune to be named by the speaker as a member of the Republican steering committee. And I was only the only freshman congressman named on that committee.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And I have a picture of our, with Speaker Joe Martin, in which we he, taken with me when he made my appointment as a member of this steering committee, which was a kind of a super committee for the Republic can leadership. It was a Republican Congress, and we had a Democratic president, President Truman, on legislation. I can say that I, I made, I think, quite a few friends on both sides of the aisle and, I served on the, Committee on Agriculture.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: It was very interesting to me. There are a lot of other forestry and agricultural matters that came up on that committee. I went out with them in the summer of 1948, and we held hearings over it all over the country in connection with some of the agricultural events. We went to the various agricultural stations and so on. But the election came around that fall, and it turned into an unexpected Democratic general landslide for Truman.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: He came out. I went by the same, about the same voters. I came in later. I, determined to, By the next time I had determined that this business of being in Congress for two years, I'd either get into politics or get out of it. So two years later, instead of running for my old place in the house, I ran for the Senate in the, Republican primary, didn't make it, and thought I was out of politics.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: A couple of years later, President Eisenhower, there's quite a story connected to that appointed me to the as general counsel to the Post Office Department. I served there four years in the largest government, civil government department, general counsel and an attorney for a cabinet officer, then the postmaster general. Then in 1958, President Eisenhower named me as a member of the Interstate Commerce Commission.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: And then I was later elected chairman because we were the only agency regulatory agency selected its own chairman. I became chairman, served in the Interstate Commerce Commission, which regulates all surface transportation in the United States, and coast wide shipping barge lines to express lines, freight forwarders, pipelines, and so on. Served as a member and chairman for nine years, then retired and came back to Moscow, where I always wanted to make my home and where I had my friends like the people, like the country.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Now, all this time, my wife has been very helpful to me. And, I have a couple of bills, a couple of speeches here, copies of a couple of speeches that I made while I was in Congress. One of them is on the air Supremacy Act. It's a short one. Another is a speech that I made, representing the state of Idaho.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: I made it to, to Valley Forge. There in Valley Forge Chapel. They have one service a year there, named for a state. And they get somebody that speaks for that state. At least they did that. No, I don't know where they do it. Now, I have here a copy of speech probably my best effort, I think, that I gave at Valley Forge in 1947.

speaker: Abe MacGregor Goff
words: Keep watch for Liberty. I have the speech I made on the Air Supremacy Act in, in the Congress on November 1947. And I also have here a speech made by my dear wife Florence here in Moscow, telling of there's social sides, lights, social side lights of Washington, D.C.. I think these might be of interest, and I'm happy to turn them over, that's all.
